# How Implicit Bias in Education Impacts STEM

## Our Commitments to be an Anti-Racist Company â

The Unruly team stands against the discrimination and violence against Black communities in recent days and throughout history. We affirm Black lives matter, and we must take action against systemic racism.   
  
We know that as a company, we are not doing enough to support Black Lives Matter and anti-racism. We want to start by committing to both improving our own internal policies to combat discrimination and implicit bias in education, and to provide external resources for our community of educators and students. We want to align our values and policies to actively work against structural racism and bias in STEM, K-12 education, and society overall. Black students too frequently lack access to the educational opportunities and resources that we find so incredibly valuable. As a growing startup, we are in a unique position to make these changes quickly and set these expectations for our company from the very beginning.

**At Unruly, we commit to making the following changes to our internal policies:**  
  
1) Implement a blind application and job screening process  
  
2) Work with local Black community groups to ensure diverse applicant pool for our team, advisors, and board  
  
3) Prioritize anti-racist education for our team members. This includes recurring, externally-led diversity dialogues for all team members and bias training for all new hires.

4) Develop race and gender equity goals for our company demographics-- and a scorecard to track them.

We want to take this time to listen to our community members regarding actions we can take to support Black educators, students, and administrators that will lead to the end of implicit bias in education. We strive to be not only a non-racist company, but an anti-racist company. If you have feedback for us, we welcome you to email us at [education@unruly-studios.com](mailto:Education@unruly-studios.com).

## Inequities in STEM and Computer ScienceÂ

Careers in STEM and computer science continue to be in high demand, which is why itâs so important for students to get high quality STEM education opportunities, starting in elementary school. Despite efforts to make STEM a priority in school, [research shows that](https://mit-teaching-systems-lab.github.io/unconscious-bias/#teaching) discrimination and implicit bias persists in education, especially in STEM and computer science. These factors contribute to lower graduation rates for Black and minority students as well as an underrepresentation of minorities in STEM and computer science careers.  
  
We take our responsibility of advocating for diversity in STEM very seriously and we believe it starts with examining inequities in STEM and the systems that perpetuate them. We are in a unique position to work with students and educators to help counteract this through addressing things like implicit bias in education. Although we understand that addressing bias in STEM is not enough to counteract the structural racism in education and overall society, we believe itâs a step in the right direction.

## What is the opportunity gap?

According to data from the [Pew Research Center](https://www.pewsocialtrends.org/2018/01/09/diversity-in-the-stem-workforce-varies-widely-across-jobs/#:~:text=Blacks%20make%20up%2011%25%20of,6%25%20of%20the%20STEM%20workforce.), Black and Hispanic people and women continue to be underrepresented in the STEM workforce. Why is this? There could be a number of reasons, but one area of research points to a lack of learning opportunities for black and other minority students. [Research shows](https://www.thecommonwealthinstitute.org/2017/10/26/unequal-opportunities-fewer-resources-worse-outcomes-for-students-in-schools-with-concentrated-poverty/) that students in high-poverty, predominantly Black and Hispanic neighborhoods have far fewer resources and less access to advanced placement STEM classes. Lack of educational opportunity accompanies the other challenges that come from generations of racist policies: housing, healthcare, and food security (just to name a few).Â    
  
Commonly referred to as the [âachievement gapâ](https://www.gse.upenn.edu/news/rethinking-achievement-gap), this lack of option and educational opportunity results in aÂ  disparity in test scores between white students and their Black and Hispanic peers. This gap exists across subjects, but it is especially prevalent in STEM and computer science. According to a survey done by [OnPoint Radio](https://www.wbur.org/onpoint/2019/09/09/achievement-gap-opportunity-education-schools-students-teachers) of their listeners, 95% of respondents say they see an achievement gap in their school districts.   
  
Along with fighting to change [school disciplinary policies](https://www.tolerance.org/magazine/spring-2013/the-school-to-prison-pipeline) that predominantly affect Black students and [high-stakes testing policies](https://cepa.stanford.edu/content/left-behind-effect-no-child-left-behind-academic-achievement-gaps), educators must examine their own implicit bias in education that could be perpetuating the inherently racist ideas these policies promote.

## What is unconscious (or implicit) bias?

Unconscious biases are the attitudes or stereotypes we hold that affect our actions and decisions in an unconscious way. Usually these biases manifest around race, ethnicity, age, gender, and sexual orientation. As a result, it leads people, organizations, and the media to negatively discriminate against minority groups without even being aware of it.Â

For example, [studies show that](https://www.pnas.org/content/109/41/16474.full) university faculty members displayed significant bias against female students and against all students of color when evaluating prospective students and staff. Both male and female faculty members exhibited this bias.

According to the [Kirwan Institute for the Study of Race and Ethnicity](http://kirwaninstitute.osu.edu/research/understanding-implicit-bias/) at Ohio State University, implicit biases have a few key characteristics:

## How can I help address unconscious bias?

You can do a lot! Although many current and past policies fail to support students of color, there is a lot educators can do in their own schools and classrooms. We encourage you to do more research on this subject (we are including links to helpful articles at the bottom of this post) but hereâs a few ideas from [CSteachingtips](https://www.csteachingtips.org/tips-reducing-bias) to get started: 1. **Know your Bias:** The first step in combating bias is to know and acknowledge your bias. Harvard has a number of [Implicit Association Tests (IAT)](https://implicit.harvard.edu/implicit/selectatest.html) that take 10 minutes - we highly recommend taking them!Â

2. **Make Explicit Expectations:** Make your expectations and grading schema clear so students know what is expected of them. You could even post it on your wall so students can refer back to it if they forget or ask you questions if they donât understand something  
  
3. **Grade Anonymously:** Remove unconscious bias from the grading by having students use their student ID numbers on their assignments, rather than their names.Â

4. **Teach students about bias:** Help your students understand what implicit bias in education is so they can recognize it when they see it. It also sets a standard for your students to expect to be treated fairly in your classroom. Southern Poverty Law Centerâs Teaching Tolerance

5. **Do Your Own Research on Bias:** Learn and reflect on your own bias by reading different research articles on the subject. See our list below for a few great resources to get started.   
  
**Implicit Bias Articles:**   
  
[**Unconscious bias in teaching**](https://mit-teaching-systems-lab.github.io/unconscious-bias/#teaching)

[**Tips For Reducing Bias in CS**](https://www.csteachingtips.org/tips-reducing-bias)[**Harvard EdCast: Unconscious Bias in Schools**](https://www.gse.harvard.edu/news/19/11/harvard-edcast-unconscious-bias-schools)

[**Implicit Bias in STEM**](https://serc.carleton.edu/sage2yc/musings/implicit_bias.html#:~:text=Extensive%20research%20has%20documented%20that,of%20us%20who%20belong%20to)[**Inequities in Computing - Open Letter**](https://blacksincomputingopenlettercommunity.wordpress.com/)[**Taking a Look Underground: Detecting, Interpreting, and Reacting to Implicit Racial Bias**](https://guilfordjournals.com/doi/pdfplus/10.1521/soco.19.4.395.20759)[**Field Experiments on Discrimination**](http://economics.mit.edu/files/11449)**Achievement Gap Articles:**[**Achievement Gap, Or Opportunity Gap? What's Stopping Student Success**](https://www.wbur.org/onpoint/2019/09/09/achievement-gap-opportunity-education-schools-students-teachers)

[**Rethinking the Achievement Gap**](https://www.gse.upenn.edu/news/rethinking-achievement-gap) **Diversity in STEM Articles:  
â** [**Diversity in the STEM workforce varies widely across jobs**](https://www.pewsocialtrends.org/2018/01/09/diversity-in-the-stem-workforce-varies-widely-across-jobs/#:~:text=Blacks%20make%20up%2011%25%20of,6%25%20of%20the%20STEM%20workforce.)**Have more resources you think we should add? Email us at education@unruly-studios.com and weâd be happy to include them!Â**

![SpringerOpen](/static/images/springeropen/logo-springer-open-d04c3ea16c.svg)

# Implicit gender stereotypes in STEM: measuring cognitive bias and group differences through reaction times

[*International Journal of STEM Education*](/)
**volume 12**, Article number: 20 (2025)
[Cite this article](#citeas)

2183 Accesses

1 Citations

10 Altmetric

[Metrics details](/articles/10.1186/s40594-025-00541-7/metrics)

## Abstract

### Background

Closing the gender gap in science, technology, engineering, and mathematics (STEM) disciplines is essential for achieving social equity and advancing innovation and socioeconomic development, as outlined in the Agenda 2030 for Sustainable Development. Despite notable progress, persistent stereotypes continue to restrict women’s aspirations and limit their access to traditionally male-dominated STEM careers, thereby influencing their social cognition and decision-making. This study examines implicit gender–career biases among STEM students by analyzing reaction times (RTs) from the implicit association test (IAT) while investigating how these biases vary across sexes and gender identities, with particular attention to their disproportionate impact on women.

### Results

A quantitative, cross-sectional experimental design was employed. The IAT was administered with blocks 3 and 4 representing stereotype‐consistent tasks and blocks 6 and 7 representing stereotype‐inconsistent tasks. In the sex-based analysis, participants classified as female exhibited significant differences between blocks 3 vs. 6 (*p* < *0.001)* and blocks 4 vs. 7 (*p* < *0.001*), whereas those classified as male showed a significant difference only between blocks 4 vs. 7 (*p* < *0.001*). In the gender-based analysis, respondents who self-identified as women demonstrated significant differences between blocks 3 vs. 6 (*p* = *0.001*) and blocks 4 vs. 7 (*p* < *0.001*), while participants who self-identified as men exhibited a significant difference only for blocks 4 vs. 7 (*p* < *0.001*). Non-binary participants showed significant differences for both comparisons, blocks 3 vs. 6 (*p* = *0.004*) and blocks 4 vs. 7 (*p* = *0.049*). In contrast, individuals reporting other gender identities exhibited a significant difference only between blocks 4 vs. 7 (*p* = *0.015*).

### Conclusions

The findings reveal that implicit gender–career associations disproportionately affect both individuals classified as female and those identifying as women or non-binary, shaping their perceptions and attitudes toward STEM. This underscores the urgent need for targeted educational interventions to mitigate these biases and promote gender equity in higher education. Addressing these cognitive barriers can enable institutions to enhance the retention and success of underrepresented groups, ultimately harnessing the full potential of diverse talent in STEM.

## Introduction

Within the framework of the 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development, it is crucial to address the gender gap in science, technology, engineering, and mathematics (STEM) careers, where women represent only 28.2% of the workforce (England et al., [2020](/articles/10.1186/s40594-025-00541-7#ref-CR33 "England, P., Levine, A., & Mishel, E. (2020). Progress toward gender equality in the United States has slowed or stalled. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America, 117(13), 6990–6997. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1918891117
                  
                "); World Economic Forum, [2024](/articles/10.1186/s40594-025-00541-7#ref-CR84 "Quadlin, N. (2018). The mark of a woman’s record: Gender and academic performance in hiring. American Sociological Review, 83(2), 331–360. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1177/0003122418762291
                  
                ")). This inequality is not only due to explicit barriers but also to implicit stereotypical beliefs deeply rooted in social cognition, which unconsciously limit women's aspirations and opportunities in these fields (Cvencek et al., [2020](/articles/10.1186/s40594-025-00541-7#ref-CR23 "Cvencek, D., Paz-Albo, J., Master, A., Herranz Llácer, C. V., Hervás-Escobar, A., & Meltzoff, A. N. (2020). Math is for me: A field intervention to strengthen math self-concepts in Spanish-speaking 3rd grade children. Frontiers in Psychology. 
                  https://doi.org/10.3389/fpsyg.2020.593995
                  
                "); Zając et al., [2024](/articles/10.1186/s40594-025-00541-7#ref-CR81 "Pelegrina, S., Martín-Puga, M. E., Lechuga, M. T., Justicia-Galiano, M. J., & Linares, R. (2024). Role of executive functions in the relations of state- and trait-math anxiety with math performance. Annals of the New York Academy of Sciences, 1535(1), 76–91. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1111/nyas.15140
                  
                ")). These automatic beliefs reinforce the tendency to view men as more agents (self-sufficient and assertive) and women as more communal (cooperative and caregivers), which discourages women from pursuing careers perceived as demanding agent traits (Luo et al., [2021](/articles/10.1186/s40594-025-00541-7#ref-CR61 "Luo, T., So, W. W. M., Wan, Z. H., & Li, W. C. (2021). STEM stereotypes predict students’ STEM career interest via self-efficacy and outcome expectations. International Journal of STEM Education, 8(1), 36. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1186/s40594-021-00295-y
                  
                "); Sczesny et al., [2018](/articles/10.1186/s40594-025-00541-7#ref-CR81 "Pelegrina, S., Martín-Puga, M. E., Lechuga, M. T., Justicia-Galiano, M. J., & Linares, R. (2024). Role of executive functions in the relations of state- and trait-math anxiety with math performance. Annals of the New York Academy of Sciences, 1535(1), 76–91. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1111/nyas.15140
                  
                ")). Automatic gender associations generate status beliefs about an individual's relative worth and competence based solely on their gender (Lacoviello et al., [2024](/articles/10.1186/s40594-025-00541-7#ref-CR59 "Lacoviello, V., Valsecchi, G., Vétois, M., & Falomir-Pichastor, J. M. (2024). Reducing the gender gap on adolescents’ interest in study fields: The impact of perceived changes in ingroup gender norms and gender prototypicality. Social Psychology of Education. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1007/s11218-024-09909-z
                  
                "); Merayo & Ayuso, [2024](/articles/10.1186/s40594-025-00541-7#ref-CR66 "Merayo, N., & Ayuso, A. (2024). Identifying beliefs about the gender gap in engineering professions among university students using community detection algorithms and statistical analysis. Computer Applications in Engineering Education. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1002/cae.22751
                  
                ")), perpetuating gender disparities in university careers, such as STEM, that continue to be reproduced (Corrigan et al., [2023](/articles/10.1186/s40594-025-00541-7#ref-CR18 "Corrigan, E., Williams, M., & Wells, M. A. (2023). High school enrolment choices—Understanding the STEM Gender Gap. Canadian Journal of Science, Mathematics and Technology Education, 23(3), 403–421. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1007/s42330-023-00285-y
                  
                "); Kayan-Fadlelmula et al., [2022](/articles/10.1186/s40594-025-00541-7#ref-CR54 "Kayan-Fadlelmula, F., Sellami, A., Abdelkader, N., & Umer, S. (2022). A systematic review of STEM education research in the GCC countries: Trends, gaps and barriers. International Journal of STEM Education, 9(1), 2. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1186/s40594-021-00319-7
                  
                ")).

Gender stereotypes are a shared set of beliefs about the attributes that characterize members of a social category (Eagly & Koenig, [2021](/articles/10.1186/s40594-025-00541-7#ref-CR29 "Eagly, A. H., & Koenig, A. M. (2021). The vicious cycle linking stereotypes and social roles. Current Directions in Psychological Science, 30(4), 343–350. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1177/09637214211013775
                  
                "); Froehlich et al., [2020](/articles/10.1186/s40594-025-00541-7#ref-CR37 "Froehlich, L., Olsson, M. I. T., Dorrough, A. R., & Martiny, S. E. (2020). Gender at work across nations: Men and women working in male-dominated and female-dominated occupations are differentially associated with agency and communion. Journal of Social Issues, 76(3), 484–511. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1111/josi.12390
                  
                ")), constituting a cognitive bias that includes an affective–evaluative load and behavioral tendencies (Fiske, [2018](/articles/10.1186/s40594-025-00541-7#ref-CR36 "Fiske, S. T. (2018). Stereotype content: Warmth and competence endure. Current Directions in Psychological Science, 27(2), 67–73. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1177/0963721417738825
                  
                ")). These stereotypes generate the perception that women are less talented and competent in scientific and technological areas than men (Napp & Breda, [2022](/articles/10.1186/s40594-025-00541-7#ref-CR72 "Napp, C., & Breda, T. (2022). The stereotype that girls lack talent: A worldwide investigation. Science Advances, 8(10), 3689. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1126/sciadv.abm3689
                  
                "); Vos et al., [2023](/articles/10.1186/s40594-025-00541-7#ref-CR81 "Pelegrina, S., Martín-Puga, M. E., Lechuga, M. T., Justicia-Galiano, M. J., & Linares, R. (2024). Role of executive functions in the relations of state- and trait-math anxiety with math performance. Annals of the New York Academy of Sciences, 1535(1), 76–91. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1111/nyas.15140
                  
                ")), affecting their self-confidence and leadership skills (Chan, [2022](/articles/10.1186/s40594-025-00541-7#ref-CR12 "Chan, R. C. H. (2022). A social cognitive perspective on gender disparities in self-efficacy, interest, and aspirations in science, technology, engineering, and mathematics (STEM): The influence of cultural and gender norms. International Journal of STEM Education, 9(1), 37. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1186/s40594-022-00352-0
                  
                "); Eagly & Karau, [2002](/articles/10.1186/s40594-025-00541-7#ref-CR28 "Eagly, A. H., & Karau, S. J. (2002). Role congruity theory of prejudice toward female leaders. Psychological Review, 109(3), 573–598. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1037/0033-295X.109.3.573
                  
                "); Jiang et al., [2024](/articles/10.1186/s40594-025-00541-7#ref-CR52 "Jiang, H., Zhang, L., & Zhang, W. (2024). Influence of career awareness on STEM career interests: Examining the roles of self-efficacy, outcome expectations, and gender. International Journal of STEM Education, 11(1), 22. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1186/s40594-024-00482-7
                  
                ")). Women are expected to display relational traits, such as warmth and care, whereas men are associated with attributes of agency such as independence and determination (Batz-Barbarich et al., [2024](/articles/10.1186/s40594-025-00541-7#ref-CR5 "Batz-Barbarich, C., Strah, N., & Tay, L. (2024). The impact of changing engineering perceptions on women’s attitudes and behavioral intentions towards engineering pursuits. International Journal of STEM Education, 11(1), 23. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1186/s40594-024-00476-5
                  
                ")), which influence social beliefs and choices (Jaoul-Grammare, [2024](/articles/10.1186/s40594-025-00541-7#ref-CR50 "Jaoul-Grammare, M. (2024). Gendered professions, prestigious professions: When stereotypes condition career choices. European Journal of Education, 59(2), e12603. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1111/ejed.12603
                  
                "); Risman & Davis, [2013](/articles/10.1186/s40594-025-00541-7#ref-CR84 "Quadlin, N. (2018). The mark of a woman’s record: Gender and academic performance in hiring. American Sociological Review, 83(2), 331–360. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1177/0003122418762291
                  
                ")). Beliefs that can operate at both unconscious and conscious levels (Greenwald & Lai, [2020](/articles/10.1186/s40594-025-00541-7#ref-CR41 "Greenwald, A. G., & Lai, C. K. (2020). Implicit social cognition. Annual Review of Psychology, 71(1), 419–445. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1146/annurev-psych-010419-050837
                  
                ")) are assumed to be true based on socialization, although they lack scientific validation (Leung et al., [2002](/articles/10.1186/s40594-025-00541-7#ref-CR60 "Leung, K., Bond, M. H., de Carrasquel, S. R., Muñoz, C., Hernández, M., Murakami, F., Yamaguchi, S., Bierbrauer, G., & Singelis, T. M. (2002). Social axioms: The search for universal dimensions of general beliefs about how the world functions. Journal of Cross-Cultural Psychology, 33(3), 286–302. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1177/0022022102033003005
                  
                ")). This difference between implicit and explicit beliefs creates challenges for research, because people may not be aware of the influence of their beliefs on their decisions (Barth & Masters, [2024](/articles/10.1186/s40594-025-00541-7#ref-CR4 "Barth, J. M., & Masters, S. (2024). In Their own words: Re-examining gender differences in career interests and motivations in a new generation. Sex Roles. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1007/s11199-024-01536-4
                  
                ")). The study of these beliefs is crucial for understanding how stereotypes affect career choices (Cvencek et al., [2023](/articles/10.1186/s40594-025-00541-7#ref-CR22 "Cvencek, D., Brečić, R., Sanders, E. A., Gaćeša, D., Skala, D., & Meltzoff, A. N. (2023). Am I a good person? Academic correlates of explicit and implicit self-esteem during early childhood. Child Development, 00, 1–16. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1111/cdev.14052
                  
                ")).

### Implicit beliefs and gender gap in STEM

Current studies have adopted various approaches to analyze implicit and explicit beliefs, which are essential for understanding the mechanisms that perpetuate gender gaps in STEM (Greenwald & Lai, [2020](/articles/10.1186/s40594-025-00541-7#ref-CR41 "Greenwald, A. G., & Lai, C. K. (2020). Implicit social cognition. Annual Review of Psychology, 71(1), 419–445. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1146/annurev-psych-010419-050837
                  
                ")). Although progress has been made in researching how these beliefs influence motivation and career decisions, significant gaps remain in understanding the specific role of implicit associations between gender and competence in these fields (Master, [2021](/articles/10.1186/s40594-025-00541-7#ref-CR64 "Master, A. (2021). Gender stereotypes influence children’s STEM motivation. Child Development Perspectives, 15(3), 203–210. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1111/cdep.12424
                  
                "); Master et al., [2023](/articles/10.1186/s40594-025-00541-7#ref-CR65 "Master, A., Tang, D., Forsythe, D., Alexander, T. M., Cheryan, S., & Meltzoff, A. N. (2023). Gender equity and motivational readiness for computational thinking in early childhood. Early Childhood Research Quarterly, 64, 242–254. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecresq.2023.03.004
                  
                ")). While it is acknowledged that these beliefs contribute to the construction of identity and perception of professional opportunities (Cvencek et al., [2023](/articles/10.1186/s40594-025-00541-7#ref-CR22 "Cvencek, D., Brečić, R., Sanders, E. A., Gaćeša, D., Skala, D., & Meltzoff, A. N. (2023). Am I a good person? Academic correlates of explicit and implicit self-esteem during early childhood. Child Development, 00, 1–16. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1111/cdev.14052
                  
                "); Eccles & Wigfield, [2020](/articles/10.1186/s40594-025-00541-7#ref-CR31 "Eccles, J. S., & Wigfield, A. (2020). From expectancy-value theory to situated expectancy-value theory: A developmental, social cognitive, and sociocultural perspective on motivation. Contemporary Educational Psychology, 61, 101859. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cedpsych.2020.101859
                  
                ")), there is still insufficient evidence to clarify how these processes limit women’s participation in STEM. In a context where gender roles impose expectations that restrict learning potential and access to these disciplines, a deeper investigation into implicit beliefs is imperative to address persistent gaps (Cross et al., [2023](/articles/10.1186/s40594-025-00541-7#ref-CR21 "Cross, C. P., Boothroyd, L. G., & Jefferson, C. A. (2023). Agent-based models of the cultural evolution of occupational gender roles. Royal Society Open Science, 10(6), 221346. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1098/rsos.221346
                  
                "); Quadlin, [2018](/articles/10.1186/s40594-025-00541-7#ref-CR84 "Quadlin, N. (2018). The mark of a woman’s record: Gender and academic performance in hiring. American Sociological Review, 83(2), 331–360. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1177/0003122418762291
                  
                ")).

Stereotypical implicit beliefs are formed in childhood and persist due to exposure to cultural norms and media (Ellemers, [2018](/articles/10.1186/s40594-025-00541-7#ref-CR32 "Ellemers, N. (2018). Gender stereotypes. Annual Review of Psychology, 69, 275–298. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1146/annurev-psych-122216-011719
                  
                "); Ward & Grower, [2020](/articles/10.1186/s40594-025-00541-7#ref-CR84 "Quadlin, N. (2018). The mark of a woman’s record: Gender and academic performance in hiring. American Sociological Review, 83(2), 331–360. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1177/0003122418762291
                  
                ")). This process involves both affinity toward one's own group and early perception of social hierarchy (Kurdi & Dunham, [2022](/articles/10.1186/s40594-025-00541-7#ref-CR58 "Kurdi, B., & Dunham, Y. (2022). What can the implicit social cognition literature teach us about implicit social cognition? The Behavioral and Brain Sciences, 45, e80. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1017/S0140525X21000595
                  
                ")). While these beliefs can influence perceptions and behaviors, they can be modified through interventions such as exposure to counter-stereotypical content (Boseovski et al., [2016](/articles/10.1186/s40594-025-00541-7#ref-CR9 "Boseovski, J. J., Hughes, C., & Miller, S. E. (2016). Expertise in unexpected places: Children’s acceptance of information from gender counter-stereotypical experts. Journal of Experimental Child Psychology, 141, 161–176. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jecp.2015.09.002
                  
                "); Hains, [2024](/articles/10.1186/s40594-025-00541-7#ref-CR45 "Hains, R. C. (2024). The Erasure of Counter-Stereotypical Female Characters From Disney’s Transmedia Toys: Exploring Toy, Media, and Audience Tensions. En The Routledge Companion to Girls’ Studies.")). Explicit beliefs, on the other hand, are more adaptable, since they are directly influenced by the sociocultural context (Coyne et al., [2014](/articles/10.1186/s40594-025-00541-7#ref-CR20 "Coyne, S. M., Linder, J. R., Rasmussen, E. E., Nelson, D. A., & Collier, K. M. (2014). It’s a bird! It’s a plane! It’s a gender stereotype!: Longitudinal associations between superhero viewing and gender stereotyped play. Sex Roles: A Journal of Research, 70(9–10), 416–430. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1007/s11199-014-0374-8
                  
                "); Zając et al., [2024](/articles/10.1186/s40594-025-00541-7#ref-CR81 "Pelegrina, S., Martín-Puga, M. E., Lechuga, M. T., Justicia-Galiano, M. J., & Linares, R. (2024). Role of executive functions in the relations of state- and trait-math anxiety with math performance. Annals of the New York Academy of Sciences, 1535(1), 76–91. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1111/nyas.15140
                  
                ")), although they can also reinforce prejudice. Thus, implicit and explicit beliefs have different origins and developmental trajectories (Amodio & Cikara, [2021](/articles/10.1186/s40594-025-00541-7#ref-CR2 "Amodio, D. M., & Cikara, M. (2021). The social neuroscience of prejudice. Annual Review of Psychology, 72, 439–469. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1146/annurev-psych-010419-050928
                  
                ")).

Implicit stereotypical beliefs are critical to understanding how gender biases affect women's participation and persistence in STEM careers (Bowman et al., [2022](/articles/10.1186/s40594-025-00541-7#ref-CR10 "Bowman, N. A., Logel, C., LaCosse, J., Jarratt, L., Canning, E. A., Emerson, K. T. U., & Murphy, M. C. (2022). Gender representation and academic achievement among STEM-interested students in college STEM courses. Journal of Research in Science Teaching, 59(10), 1876–1900. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1002/tea.21778
                  
                "), Pedersen and Nielsen, [2024](/articles/10.1186/s40594-025-00541-7#ref-CR80 "Pedersen, J. V., & Nielsen, M. W. (2024). Gender, self-efficacy and attrition from STEM programmes: Evidence from Danish survey and registry data. Studies in Higher Education, 49(1), 47–61. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1080/03075079.2023.2220702
                  
                ")). These subconscious beliefs significantly influence people’s decisions and behaviors (Greenwald & Lai, [2020](/articles/10.1186/s40594-025-00541-7#ref-CR41 "Greenwald, A. G., & Lai, C. K. (2020). Implicit social cognition. Annual Review of Psychology, 71(1), 419–445. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1146/annurev-psych-010419-050837
                  
                ")). Implicit beliefs can determine women's expectations and aspirations in academic contexts (Nicolleau et al., [2022](/articles/10.1186/s40594-025-00541-7#ref-CR73 "Nicolleau, M., Mascret, N., Martha, C., Naude, C., Serre, T., & Ragot-Court, I. (2022). Implicit theories in driving: Scale development, validation, and predictive role on violations and driving self-efficacy. Transportation Research Part F: Traffic Psychology and Behaviour, 86, 333–344. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.trf.2022.03.003
                  
                ")), perpetuating gender inequality in male-dominated fields (Moè et al., [2021](/articles/10.1186/s40594-025-00541-7#ref-CR68 "Moè, A., Hausmann, M., & Hirnstein, M. (2021). Gender stereotypes and incremental beliefs in STEM and non-STEM students in three countries: Relationships with performance in cognitive tasks. Psychological Research Psychologische Forschung, 85(2), 554–567. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1007/s00426-019-01285-0
                  
                ")). For example, automatic associations between gender and competence can lead to the underestimation of women's skills in scientific and technological disciplines, affecting their self-esteem, self-efficacy, and performance (Nix et al., [2015](/articles/10.1186/s40594-025-00541-7#ref-CR75 "Nix, S., Perez-Felkner, L., & Thomas, K. (2015). Perceived mathematical ability under challenge: A longitudinal perspective on sex segregation among STEM degree fields. Frontiers in Psychology. 
                  https://doi.org/10.3389/fpsyg.2015.00530
                  
                "); Petzel et al., [2024](/articles/10.1186/s40594-025-00541-7#ref-CR81 "Pelegrina, S., Martín-Puga, M. E., Lechuga, M. T., Justicia-Galiano, M. J., & Linares, R. (2024). Role of executive functions in the relations of state- and trait-math anxiety with math performance. Annals of the New York Academy of Sciences, 1535(1), 76–91. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1111/nyas.15140
                  
                ")). These biases not only restrict opportunities for women but also deprive institutions of fully exploiting available talent (Keller et al., [2022](/articles/10.1186/s40594-025-00541-7#ref-CR55 "Keller, L., Preckel, F., Eccles, J. S., & Brunner, M. (2022). Top-performing math students in 82 countries: An integrative data analysis of gender differences in achievement, achievement profiles, and achievement motivation. Journal of Educational Psychology, 114(5), 966–991. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1037/edu0000685
                  
                ")). Therefore, it is crucial to identify and address implicit beliefs to promote greater equity and diversity in STEM.

This study measured implicit beliefs among STEM college students using the implicit association test (IAT), a population that has been underexplored in previous research. While the IAT has been widely used to investigate biases in teachers (Sabin et al., [2022](/articles/10.1186/s40594-025-00541-7#ref-CR84 "Quadlin, N. (2018). The mark of a woman’s record: Gender and academic performance in hiring. American Sociological Review, 83(2), 331–360. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1177/0003122418762291
                  
                ")), high school students (Jain et al., [2022](/articles/10.1186/s40594-025-00541-7#ref-CR49 "Jain, T., Dhar, D., Kapoor, V., Kapur, V., & Raj, A. (2022). Measuring gender attitudes: Developing and testing implicit association tests for adolescents in India. PLoS ONE, 17(6), e0264077. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0264077
                  
                ")), and graduate students (Kramer et al., [2021](/articles/10.1186/s40594-025-00541-7#ref-CR57 "Kramer, M., Heyligers, I. C., & Könings, K. D. (2021). Implicit gender-career bias in postgraduate medical training still exists, mainly in residents and in females. BMC Medical Education, 21(1), 253. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1186/s12909-021-02694-9
                  
                ")), most studies have focused on topics, such as gender identity (Clausen et al., [2021](/articles/10.1186/s40594-025-00541-7#ref-CR15 "Clausen, S., Tajadura-Jiménez, A., Janssen, C. P., & Bianchi-Berthouze, N. (2021). Action sounds informing own body perception influence gender identity and social cognition. Frontiers in Human Neuroscience. 
                  https://doi.org/10.3389/fnhum.2021.688170
                  
                ")) or religion (Nillesen et al., [2021](/articles/10.1186/s40594-025-00541-7#ref-CR74 "Nillesen, E., Grimm, M., Goedhuys, M., Reitmann, A.-K., & Meysonnat, A. (2021). On the malleability of gender attitudes: Evidence from implicit and explicit measures in Tunisia. World Development, 138, 105263. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.worlddev.2020.105263
                  
                ")). The lack of specific research on STEM college students is notable, given the significant impact implicit biases can have on career choice and retention in these disciplines (Montgomery et al., [2024](/articles/10.1186/s40594-025-00541-7#ref-CR69 "Montgomery, B., Park, H., Barry Burrill, L., & Melamed, D. (2024). Measuring gender status beliefs. Socius, 10, 23780231241245844. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1177/23780231241245845
                  
                "); Sebastián-Tirado et al., [2023](/articles/10.1186/s40594-025-00541-7#ref-CR84 "Quadlin, N. (2018). The mark of a woman’s record: Gender and academic performance in hiring. American Sociological Review, 83(2), 331–360. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1177/0003122418762291
                  
                ")). This study provides an empirical basis for understanding how implicit beliefs influence STEM students and helps develop strategies to promote gender equity in these fields (Howell et al., [2024](/articles/10.1186/s40594-025-00541-7#ref-CR48 "Howell, J. L., Lofaro, N., & Ratliff, K. A. (2024). Responding to feedback about implicit bias. Social and Personality Psychology Compass, 18(1), e12926. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1111/spc3.12926
                  
                "); Shanks et al., [2024](/articles/10.1186/s40594-025-00541-7#ref-CR84 "Quadlin, N. (2018). The mark of a woman’s record: Gender and academic performance in hiring. American Sociological Review, 83(2), 331–360. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1177/0003122418762291
                  
                ")). By addressing these biases, students can make decisions that are more aligned with their personal values, thus mitigating automatic prejudices and fostering more inclusive, equitable environments (Marini & Banaji, [2022](/articles/10.1186/s40594-025-00541-7#ref-CR63 "Marini, M., & Banaji, M. R. (2022). An implicit gender sex-science association in the general population and STEM faculty. The Journal of General Psychology, 149(3), 299–326. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1080/00221309.2020.1853502
                  
                ")).

### Implicit bias and career decision-making

The influence of implicit biases extends beyond cognitive performance, shaping individuals' career decisions in subtle but significant ways (Visser et al., [2024](/articles/10.1186/s40594-025-00541-7#ref-CR84 "Quadlin, N. (2018). The mark of a woman’s record: Gender and academic performance in hiring. American Sociological Review, 83(2),

enriched_points.expanded_content: Implicit biases unconsciously influence educators' decisions, resulting in unequal treatment and opportunities for students, particularly affecting underrepresented groups in STEM fields.
Cultural mismatches occur when educational practices do not align with the cultural backgrounds of students, leading to disengagement and lower achievement in STEM subjects.
Underrepresentation of minorities in STEM education and careers limits mentorship opportunities, perpetuating stereotypes and discouraging students from pursuing STEM pathways.
Stereotypes associating STEM with specific genders or ethnicities create barriers, affecting self-perception and aspirations of students, particularly among women and minority groups.
